turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor. I think that it is an isolated incident. What occurred —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There is no communication between different branches of the different police departments?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: There is nothing in this record, and there is nothing to my knowledge.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought one of the opinions referred to something like that.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: There is some language in one of the D.C. opinions that says that the officer had said that. But the opinion also goes on to say that they treat that as irrelevant: what the subjective thoughts of the officer were. They do not use that as part of their holding. And in this case, we need to decide what a reasonable person in Mr. Bostick's circumstances would have thought when two people in plain clothes came, actually one person came up to talk to him, the other officer was further up in the bus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Have we ever said in our cases that the exercise of your rights is grounds for additional suspicion on the part of officers?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: No, we have not. Pardon me, no, you have not. This Court has not so held. And we are not saying that here today. What we are saying is that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you are not. Because you responded to Justice Stevens, that this would not be grounds for giving added suspicion to either these police or to police in another jurisdiction. So I think that is consistent with your answer.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it be consistent with your position for the police to call ahead to the next city where the bus comes in, and suggest to them that without basing any reasonable suspicion, that they just reinterrogate the same person? Would there be anything wrong with that?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I am not sure that they would have a basis for doing that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the fact that he — unlike so many thousands of people who are willing to let their bags be searched, unlike so many, he just wouldn't let them search his bag. I gather from the history of this, that most people are willing to allow their bags to be searched.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Right. What we have learned is that most people feel a moral compulsion to cooperate with police officers, especially when they are informed about the problems that we have with drugs. I just —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Problems they are not aware of until the officer has confronted them on the bus, I suppose.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Or they may well be aware of them because it is all over the media. It is all over, you know, people have — they know of people in the community that have problems. I mean, it is common knowledge that drugs are a very serious problem in this country. So I don't think that most people, when they are approached by officers, that that is the first time that they have heard about a drug problem, but that might give rise to why they are more likely to cooperate. I really can't give you a yes or no answer as to whether or not calling ahead would be proper.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why would it then — are you urging a "no harm in asking" rule? I mean, do you need probable cause or anything just to come up and say, do you mind if I search your bag?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: No. You don't need probable cause.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So why would there be —

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: That is merely a citizen encounter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why would there be any question about calling ahead? If you say that there is no violation of the law involved in simply asking somebody.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Right. I just have a concern that if you do this, you know, 25 times, that might be a problem. And that that question is not —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, that could be police harassment, but it has nothing to do with whether it is a violation of the Fourth Amendment, I would assume.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Okay. That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, consistently, with the rest of your case.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Correct. Thank you, Your Honor. What we need to look here, is to whether or not the actions of the officers, the totality of their actions were reasonable and whether or not they caused a restraint of liberty of Mr. Bostick. Florida asserts that there was no restraint of his liberty. There was no show of force. There was no display of a weapon. There was no display of authority. In any —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was there any showing that the bus departure was delayed?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: In the bus driver's deposition he stated that they left 20 minutes late. However, it is unclear as to whether that was a direct result of Mr. Bostick's arrest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose the police did delay the bus 20 minutes in order to conduct this sort of investigation, would that have any bearing on the case or would it change the case?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I don't think it would. I think that that would be between the bus company and the police officers, as long as —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, suppose the police delay the bus for an hour and a half, that has nothing to do with whether there is a seizure?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I think you have to look to what they are doing with each individual person on the bus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, suppose they are conducting this investigation that was conducted here, but it took them an hour and a half? And they ask the bus to please cooperate, the driver to please cooperate by waiting until they got there and they took their time to get there. They delay it for an hour and a half for this investigation.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Are you saying this investigation with Mr. Bostick alone or with everyone on the bus?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, it is the same facts as you have here.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I think that that is a factor that should be considered, but it does not necessarily give rise to a seizure. What we are saying here, and where we are saying the Supreme Court of Florida has erred is in not considering the totality of the circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Supposing, Ms. Fowler, that the bus has an accident on the highway between Ft. Lauderdale and Palm Beach and the sheriff comes and says, I want to investigate the accident. Please hold up the bus for an hour while we — I want to interview the driver. I want to take some photographs. Do you think the people on the bus are seized because of that?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: No, because that is regular police business. I don't think there is any problem at all with that. And I am not saying that they were seized under the other scenario. I am saying it is a factor to be considered. And if there is an accident, clearly the police are within their rights to do whatever investigation is required.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Fowler, backing up a little, why did they go straight to the back of the bus?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: What we have learned from the officers is that they try to approach as many people on the bus as they can within the time allotted to them. And so it is easier — 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. that all of the drug pushers, when you ask to search them, they say, oh, come on. If looks like usual people don't do that. Why is it that the guys who plead guilty — why do dope pushers plead guilty?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Because they are guilty. If you are asking why they plead guilty after they have been, after there is probable cause for arrest —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no. I mean, when I have got dope on me, and I say search me, am I not pleading guilty?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: You are cooperating with the police officers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is right. Why? Can you give me a reason why?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I think a reasonable person would do it —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They are just good people.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Because they are trying to be cooperative.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They are just good people?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is the reasonable dope dealer we are talking about.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: But the test that we need to be looking at is that of a reasonable citizen. What would a reasonable citizen

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But as Justice Marshall points out, the cases always are people who are not reasonable citizens. May I just ask on the standard practice. Is it standard practice to search as many bags as possible during the stop at the —

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: It is standard practice to approach as many people and request consent for the search of the bags as possible.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How many did they search?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: On this bag, excuse me, on this bus they never got past Mr. Bostick. Because once they arrested him it was time for the bus to move on. I think, Justice Marshall, in response to your question, I am sure there are a lot of drug dealers who do say no, and we just never see those cases. So it is not as if every drug dealer is saying, is agreeing to this for some reason. If there are no further questions —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They may not be a very bright bunch of people perhaps.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Yes, that is probably very true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which is a good thing, I suppose. view of that argument, on the statement in the brief amicus for the Americans for Effective Law Enforcement, Professor Envow's group, which has uniformly supported the Government in all of these cases. It says, "Amicus, with more than 16 years experience in police training and education, view the confrontation and search procedure used here as highly unusual, it not unique." Do you think he is wrong?

turns.name: KENNETH W. STARR
turns.text: It is only unusual in the sense that it is relatively new. Broward County began implementing this in the early 1980's. This jurisdiction began implementing it 3 years ago. All Federal courts of appeals that have now been confronted with the legality of this have unanimously sustained it. No dissents whatever. The Fourth Circuit's opinion in Flowers, in particular, speaking the Fourth Circuit speaking through Judge Wilkinson made, I think, a pivotal point. This program is not only valuable in assuring the interdiction of drugs; but it is also valuable in terms of depriving individuals of dangerous weapons. That is one aspect of this program. And the results in Broward County are quite consistent with those in the District of Columbia and elsewhere. Not only are drugs seized, but weapons are seized as well; 45 dangerous weapons were seized by Broward County in the space of a year and a half. In terms of the procedures that are employed, no, I don't think that they are intimidating, and I think that for this reason. All these police officers engaged in this program are trained. They have read this Court's cases. They know that they cannot be confrontational. They know they cannot demand cooperation. They know, however, under this Court's cases, they can go to a citizen and ask for that citizen's cooperation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: General Starr?

turns.name: KENNETH W. STARR
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If there had been a shooting match on that bus, there would have been a whole lot of people killed, wouldn't there?

turns.name: KENNETH W. STARR
turns.text: There certainly might have been.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And who would have been responsible for it?

turns.name: KENNETH W. STARR
turns.text: Those who began the firing, I would think. Justice Marshall, let me give you this assurance: not only is this record barren of any indication of such a problem, it is our experience that there is no such problem. There have been no such incidents to our knowledge of shoot-outs on buses. Individuals cooperate. And why do they cooperate? This Court, by the way, has in Schneckloth and in Mendenhall and in Delgado, said, it is irrelevant whether you have been told whether you are free to cooperate or not. The point is, this is a free society. You have the right to say no. This Court has had cases before it in which individuals have asserted that right. They have said no. Brown against Texas, the police offices come to the individual and he says, I am not goings to cooperate. At that point, the police officers in this program are trained to back off. Whether they must, in response to Justice Scalia's question, is a different matter in terms of Fourth Amendment analysis, because the critical Fourth Amendment inquiry is whether, as General Fowler put it, under the totality of the circumstances, there has been a show of force, a display of force or a show of authority so as to restrain the individual. I have no problem at all with the concept that Justice Souter suggested of detention. The police 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 15 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. 1111 FOURTEENTH STREET, N.W. ALDERSON REPORTING COMPANY, INC. ALDERSON REPORTING COMPANY, INC. 14 in the affirmative." They decided this on the basis of a the normal person is going to say — ALDERSON REPORTING COMPANY, INC. foot or two hardly to move in. In the factory, the opinion for the Court made clear that there is an opportunity to move around the factory.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they weren't free to leave it, were they?

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: No. But I think the question is, would a reasonable person feel that they were put in a situation where they were detained, that is to say, their physical freedom, to move about and to move out of the presence of the officer had been restricted. And I think where you have an entire factory to move around in, it is a far different circumstance than where you simply have no where to go at all to get away from the presence of the officer, and you are relying entirely, as the other side has argued in their brief is enough, you are relying entirely on the willingness of the officer to walk away from you because you can't go anywhere. I think it is a very, very different circumstance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Ayer, I thought you said earlier, I thought you acknowledged earlier that it is not the confinement; it is only the confinement by reason of authority, so that the mere fact that you are on a bus or anymore than the fact that you are on an elevator doesn't make it an arrest. So to be sure, he may only be able to move three seats away, but so long as he can move three seats away and there is no force or threat of force that prevents him from moving that distance or further, I don't see why it is an arrest, why it is a seizure.

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: Number one, I don't see how he could move even three seats away here. But in any event, if I understand the argument you are making, Justice Scalia, it would suggest that if there is a building that has for some reason a little nook that has three walls and somebody is leaning against the wall sunning himself and the nook is only 2 feet wide and an officer walks up to him and blocks the way out, and says, I want to talk to you —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, no. If the officer is blocking the way out, that is restricting his movement by force or threat of force. But if an officer in an elevator, the man standing in an elevator, the officer begins a conversation with him. Now he can't move very far in the elevator. Has he been seized? I would think you would say no because it has to be — the reason he can't move has to be the assertion of authority over him.

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: I would agree with that. I would agree that one would not be seized in that circumstance. I think on the other hand — on the other hand I think here, where the officers have boarded the bus, and they have approached someone who is in the back of the bus and they have acted in a way that they should know, and really must know, communicates a sense of their control, that they have to take responsibility for that. That they can't say, as is argued, you chose to put yourself on the bus.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is a good point. I am not questioning that point of your argument. I am just questioning the part of your argument that says he couldn't go far, he could only move two or three seats away. That seems to me irrelevant.

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: Our argument really is that here he had no where to go at all.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If we understood the Supreme Court of Florida as saying that in no case, under no circumstances may police board a bus and inquire from passengers about their luggage, you would not defend that rule?

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: My view is that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You say you are not asking for it?

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: We are not asking for that rule —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And furthermore, would you defend it if we understood that to be the rule?

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: I think that is broader than the rule should be. I think on the other hand that there are —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, give me the circumstances under which police may board the bus and ask a customer or a passenger, may we search your luggage?

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: Well, I think if the police —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If a small person, that couldn't block the aisle —

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: If the police go out of their way to negate what I believe to be the normal inference that a person would draw from the rather unusual, to most passengers, occurrence of an officer coming on a bus, that is to say, he is here for a reason —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if they say, don't get us wrong, you are quite free to leave the bus. Of course, you may not get to Ft. Lauderdale, but you are quite free to leave.

turns.name: DONALD B. AYER
turns.text: I think they shouldn't say that, but I think if they say, you are free to leave. This is routine questioning. You know, we have a real drug problem here and we would like to talk to you. I think my view is that that would be acceptable, as long as they do nothing else — I think that you are correct in saying that the Supreme Court of Florida has held a —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Justice Scalia, please.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: I am sorry, Justice Scalia. That there was the per se rule that under no circumstances can police go on buses to discuss with citizens, to ask their cooperation for a search for any type of contraband. And we are saying here today that that bright line rule, that test should not be the test. Also, we are saying that under the specific facts of Bostick, that there was no show of authority sufficient to restrain his liberty, so as to implicate the Fourth Amendment. The aisle was not blocked. Mr. Bostick's counsel has gotten up here and repeatedly said that the aisle was blocked. It was partially blocked. Mr. Bostick could still have gotten past —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We are a little bit in the dark about some of the facts, are we not, Ms. Fowler?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Yes, we are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In view of the — the trial court did not make specific findings.

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: That is correct. We would ask that this Court look at those facts that are set forth in the Supreme Court opinion which is in our petition for cert at A2.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which quotes from Judge Letts' dissent?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: Yes. Those facts are sufficient, I think for this Court to make a ruling. Therefore, we don't have to get into any questions about whether or not he was sleeping, and there is evidence that he was not. Just because the door to this bus was closed does not mean that the defendant could not have gotten off the bus. It could have still be opened. There is no evidence that it was locked. That would be a fire hazard or something.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you know how to open it?

turns.name: JOAN FOWLER
turns.text: My understanding is, all you have to do is push it. There is no specific levers or anything like that. A mere pushing is sufficient. And under Delgado, this Court has found that it is not a test of whether or not you are free to leave, it is whether or not you are free to go about your business. And Mr. Bostick's business was staying on the bus to continue his travels, and he did not have to talk to these officers if he did not want to. Thank you.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Ms. Fowler. The case is submitted
